message:
test(harness): conformance ext4 i_blocks vs debugfs Blockcount

`Ext4Inode.blocks` (the on-disk `i_blocks` field) records the number
of 512-byte sectors charged to an inode — including data blocks plus
any external extent / indirect-block overhead, but excluding the
inode block itself. It is the source of truth for `stat(2).st_blocks`,
so a regression that misreads it would silently corrupt every
`du -k` / `ls -s` / FUSE getattr call a downstream caller makes.

ffs has unit-test coverage of the inode parser but no end-to-end
pin against the kernel/e2fsprogs view of `i_blocks`. The existing
kernel_reference harness compares mode, size, links, mtimes,
generation and the inode checksum but never reads back this
accounting field.

New harness ext4_iblocks_kernel_reference stages a 7-case corpus
that covers the meaningful blockcount shapes:

  * 1-byte file (1 block allocated → 8 sectors);
  * exactly one block of content (8 sectors, no overhead);
  * 32 KiB / 8 contiguous blocks (64 sectors, single inline extent
    with no external block overhead);
  * sparse: 4 KiB content extended via `sif size` to 16 KiB
    (still 8 sectors — i_blocks tracks allocation, not i_size);
  * fast symlink (≤ 60-byte target stored in `i_block`,
    Blockcount = 0);
  * extent-mapped symlink (200-byte target → one allocated block,
    Blockcount = 8);
  * empty directory (single 4 KiB block → 8 sectors).

For every case the harness parses `Blockcount: N` from
`debugfs stat <path>` and asserts byte-for-byte equality with
`Ext4Inode.blocks`. Cross-corpus invariants enforce that both the
zero-blockcount path (fast symlink) and a non-zero path are
exercised, so a future cleanup that drops one shape silently
weakens coverage.

Self-skips when ext4 userland is unavailable.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
